> That's a terribly inefficient way of doing things. Just have a look at the [underlying code for the `Text` widget](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/blob/2f73173c36f806c7f30e705a90722082f85a2c8b/packages/flutter/lib/src/widgets/text.dart#L520), and you'll see why.

Thank you for your feedback.

Yes, terribly inefficient way, but it works.
I'm waiting for an oficial solution or another that works and is more efficient.

In the meantime, I'll try to make a more efficient solution.

@davidsdearaujo , if you want at least somewhat more efficient solution, look here - https://itnext.io/writing-custom-widgets-in-flutter-part-1-ellipsizedtext-a0efdc1368a8. Using TextPainter is much more efficient that widget-per-character.

Instead of using a Text-Widget for every letter, I am using this workaround for quite a while:

`Characters('your string').replaceAll(Characters(''), Characters('\u{200B}')).toString();`

You need the [characters](https://pub.dev/packages/characters) library.

I am using this approach in production for half a year and didn't have any problems. It even works with emojis.

> Instead of using a Text-Widget for every letter, I am using this workaround for quite a while:
>

>

Nice solution!

We can also use it without the `Character` class:

`'Lorem Ipsum is simply dummy text'.replaceAll('', '\u{200B}')`

> 
> Nice solution!
> 
> We can also use it without the `Character` class:
> 
> `'Lorem Ipsum is simply dummy text'.replaceAll('', '\u{200B}')`

This solution breaks when users are using emojis. That's why I use characters.

If you display only your own Strings there should be no problem.

> This solution breaks when users are using emojis. That's why I use characters.

Oh, understood.
The `character` package is already in Flutter. Now we have an extension to get the characters from `String`:
```dart
'Lorem Ipsum is simply dummy text'
    .characters
    .replaceAll(Characters(''), Characters('\u{200B}'))
    .toString()
```

Thanks you for your contribution!
It is not a solution. It's a workaround. Be very cautious with using it. It changes how characters are displayed. Differences are subtle, but overall, letters are a bit wider.

+1 here. This workaround has proven to be the best, but agreed this is an inconvenient issue that has been around far too long.

`Text(
  'Text',
  style: Styles.font.caption(),
  maxLines: 1,
  overflow: TextOverflow.fade,
  softWrap: false,
),`

> It is not a solution. It's a workaround. Be very cautious with using it. It changes how characters are displayed. Differences are subtle, but overall, letters are a bit wider.

Yes, this workaround increases letter spacing so it is not suitable for us. I just found another workaround which I not see in this thread:

```dart
 text.replaceAll(' ', '\u{000A0}')
```

We started to use it and it seems that it "just works" for all our use cases. It just replaces all spaces in text with Unicode [No-Break Space](https://unicode-table.com/en/00A0/) character.

Hope it may help someone :)

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/46816270/209114444-65da107f-9d96-4737-849b-0ffec3a7685e.png)

@roman-petrov that's a good fix. If you make one more modification it also replaces hyphens with non-breaking hyphens:

```dart
'UFC 284 - Makhachev vs Volkanovski'
    .characters
    .replaceAll(Characters(' '), Characters('\u{00A0}'))
    .replaceAll(Characters('-'), Characters('\u{2011}'))
    .toString()
```

Without the second `replaceAll` the text looks like this:

"UFC 284 -..."

Instead of this:

"UFC 284 - Makhachev vs V..."

my current workaround is to replace.

`str.replaceAll('', '\u200B')`
Hi @fullflash, if you do it that way just be wary of issues with letter spacing, emojis and hyphens.
Any status update for this issue?

> ```dart
>  .characters
>     .replaceAll(Characters(' '), Characters('\u{00A0}'))
>     .replaceAll(Characters('-'), Characters('\u{2011}'))
>     .toString()
> ```

Yes but this solutions also results incorrect breaking chars like "u" letter in word "you" as you can see in the screenshot

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/19576508/228672077-969c3c2e-ccce-4f46-a0ba-32961827230e.png)

/cc @rusino @jason-simmons @dnfield 

The Skia team is about to start looking into this.  Rough plan is something like:

* Run the line breaking/word wrap algorithm as we currently do
* If there is overflow beyond maxLines and ellipsizing is enabled, then ignore the last line break and shape the entire rest of the text as one line
* Truncate clusters in the last line until the ellipsis fits

Also, from discussion:

> In the following example, _none_ of the "bbb" word is rendered.  The goal is to instead render as much of the "bbb" word as will fit.
> 
> Or if you replace the string with "http://www.google.com", the current algorithm only renders the "http://" because that point is considered to be a soft line break.
>
> ```dart
> import 'package:flutter/material.dart';
> 
> void main() {
>   runApp(
>     Center(
>       child: SizedBox(
>         width: 200,
>         height: 100,
>         child: Text(
>           'aaaaa bbbbbbb',
>           style: TextStyle(fontSize: 30.0, color: Colors.white, backgroundColor: Colors.blue),
>           maxLines: 1,
>           overflow: TextOverflow.ellipsis,
>           textDirection: TextDirection.ltr,
>         ),
>       ),
>     ),
>   );
> }
```

@MisterLight The [changelog](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/wiki/Hotfixes-to-the-Stable-Channel#3712-apr-19-2023) for 3.7.12 doesn't mention any changes related to this problem. The fix must be a part of some upcoming release
This will not be backported to 3.7. I have requested a CP into the 3.10 beta here https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/125308.

I took a cursory glance (it's EOD Friday here so I'll have to look again next week) and in general this seems like an improvement, and in the couple of places where code owners might prefer the legacy style I suspect you could make 1-off adjustments.

That being said, I'm having a hard time understanding the state of the proposal; [the original design]([flutter.dev/go/dart-patterns](https://flutter.dev/go/dart-patterns)) has a combination of positive and negative comments, and the only other (public?) [comment I see from @goderbauer](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/150942#issuecomment-2238677144) implies not a strong amount of support.

If there were other outcomes or conversations you can share to explain this change, that would be great.

Sure! I'd be happy to give an overview of what I've learned.

Hixie engaged with the doc the most, and also was the most strongly opposed to pattern-matching out of the people who responded. His negative responses (e.g. [here](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1p6JV-pvr8_45qI5fumv6Ir60wEeagKCceTngYOXt_U4/edit?resourcekey=0-jGJyRsTPSIpGPN0xgvzSwg&disco=AAABSmXEl5s), [here](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1p6JV-pvr8_45qI5fumv6Ir60wEeagKCceTngYOXt_U4/edit?resourcekey=0-jGJyRsTPSIpGPN0xgvzSwg&disco=AAABR2UHvJA), [here](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1p6JV-pvr8_45qI5fumv6Ir60wEeagKCceTngYOXt_U4/edit?resourcekey=0-jGJyRsTPSIpGPN0xgvzSwg&disco=AAABR2UHvJM), and [here](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1p6JV-pvr8_45qI5fumv6Ir60wEeagKCceTngYOXt_U4/edit?resourcekey=0-jGJyRsTPSIpGPN0xgvzSwg&disco=AAABR2UHvJU)) are summarized really well by a comment he made [at the top](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1p6JV-pvr8_45qI5fumv6Ir60wEeagKCceTngYOXt_U4/edit?resourcekey=0-jGJyRsTPSIpGPN0xgvzSwg&disco=AAABSmXEl5c): simplicity is more important than concision. There were other spots where he gave a more neutral response ([here](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1p6JV-pvr8_45qI5fumv6Ir60wEeagKCceTngYOXt_U4/edit?resourcekey=0-jGJyRsTPSIpGPN0xgvzSwg&disco=AAABR2UHvIg), [here](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1p6JV-pvr8_45qI5fumv6Ir60wEeagKCceTngYOXt_U4/edit?resourcekey=0-jGJyRsTPSIpGPN0xgvzSwg&disco=AAABSeHEHEs), [here](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1p6JV-pvr8_45qI5fumv6Ir60wEeagKCceTngYOXt_U4/edit?resourcekey=0-jGJyRsTPSIpGPN0xgvzSwg&disco=AAABR2UHvI8), and [here](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1p6JV-pvr8_45qI5fumv6Ir60wEeagKCceTngYOXt_U4/edit?resourcekey=0-jGJyRsTPSIpGPN0xgvzSwg&disco=AAABR2UHvJE)) along the lines of "neither one is very readable." As far as his supportive feedback, one comment in particular stuck out: > i think non-pattern switch is probably simpler than a long if chain, because the reader can immediately see "ok we're going to just be comparing this one value against a bunch of other values" whereas with the if chain you have to check "is every condition the same form? are they actually chained with 'else' properly?" etc. When we start adding patterns to the switch though I think it can quickly get more complicated. > > That said, I think there are cases where even pattern switch is better than an if chain. For example if you have a sealed class hierarchy and you're checking against every option and matching every field, that's probably clearer than a bunch of ifs and casts.
Mylo Fawcett also engaged quite a bit and showed more support overall for implementing patterns. But one notable exception that I found surprising was the [firm disapproval](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1p6JV-pvr8_45qI5fumv6Ir60wEeagKCceTngYOXt_U4/edit?resourcekey=0-jGJyRsTPSIpGPN0xgvzSwg&disco=AAABSuZ9RFA) of doing an `Offset` object pattern assignment.
I was also interested to hear from Bob Nystrom that Dart pattern-matching has a lot in common with Swift syntax.

It's definitely not cut-and-dry whether pattern-matching is overall "better" or "worse" than the alternative (there are some situations where whether the pattern is more readable depends on how long the variable names are). There's a lot of variety, both with regards to how patterns would apply to a particular code snippet, and also pertaining to the individual reactions and overall opinions that different people have. That being said, there was one huge takeaway that hadn't occurred to me until I started talking with people and gathering feedback: **map patterns are far more readable than object patterns**. ```dart if (someMap case {'key': final Object value}) { // ... } ``` It's distinct, concise, and expressive: the `key` being directly adjacent to the `value` is huge.
By comparison, object patterns are often not as great, especially with the leading colon: - Multiple people have mentioned that `Size(:final double height)` looks wonky. - And `Size(height: final double height)` is more verbose and not much less confusing.

To decide what went into this PR, I basically just included all the map patterns, and then some other things that I thought looked nice. > in the couple of places where code owners might prefer the legacy style I suspect you could make 1-off adjustments. Sounds like a great plan!

@nate-thegrate I am still looking for this. To help out here is a mini example of what I am looking for. 

```
This pr adopts the following newer dart language features ["switch expressions"](https://dart.dev/language/branches#switch-expressions),  ["if case"](https://dart.dev/language/branches#if-case), ... 

Apart from migrating the code here are some situations where though code review and pr feedback we should or should not use these new language features. 

We should use switch expressions to collapse long if else statements. [example 1](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/blob/b327787d9bbacfd3c5c1bf2e5b130c3b7b38b6bd/dev/manual_tests/lib/focus.dart#L111) [example 2](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/blob/b327787d9bbacfd3c5c1bf2e5b130c3b7b38b6bd/packages/flutter/lib/src/foundation/assertions.dart#L679)
Should not use switch expressions instead of an if when dealing with and object and its sub fields simultaneously
[Example](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/pull/154753#discussion_r1784839751)

```

I am thinking of this pr as an implementation of style guide advice that has yet to be written. Additionally for reviewers that do not want to read the entire diff this is a preview of what is being approved. This pr will most likely be linked as evidence for or against style changes in the future lets make it easy to follow.
Thank you @reidbaker! I've updated the PR description and am interested to hear your thoughts.

You don't need my LGTM, but you can have it anyway for your patience and diligence.

## feat: Introduce WrapFit

As a potential solution for #136154 and #135301, I've added a `WrapFit` as a property of the `WrapParentData`. `Wrap` respects the `fit` during layout. One can wrap a child of `Wrap` with `Wrapped` in order to set change the `fit`. (Inspired by `Flex`)

The new algorithm checks if the child has either `WrapFit.runTight` or `WrapFit.runLoose`. If it has, wrap checks, whether the child's min axis size (depending on the direction) fits in the current run. If it does, the child is layout with constraints, that fit in the current run. If it doesn't, the child will receive constraints that fit in the next run.

Please have a look at the new docs, for the effect of the different values of `WrapFit`.

Demonstration of `WrapFit.runLoose`:
![wrapfit](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/assets/45403027/186a2734-5a05-4e24-8933-2046506230aa)

*I'm no native speaker. Please make sure the docs are adequate.*

Fix #136154 
Fix #135301

> I consider creating a package for `WrapFit`, till it's available in the stable release. Would there be any interest?

That would be awesome! рҹҷҸ

I've published the `wrapfit` package to [pub.dev](https://pub.dev/packages/wrapfit) for everyone who needs the `WrapFit` feature right now. *I just copied most of the code.*

Simply add the package and use `Wrap2` instead of `Wrap`.

**Please DO NOT report issues regarding the wrapfit package here.** *Use [my repo](https://github.com/fischerscode/pub.packages/issues) instead.*

(It's still my goal to get this PR merged.)

I love that this is now a package which can be independently updated and maintained by the awesome Flutter community. Since using the package in an app is pretty straightforward, what do you think about closing this PR and directing folks who want this feature to your cool new package?

**TLDR; I think this should be a part of flutter and not an extra package.**

The package is just a copy of the entire `Wrap` implementation. I did especially just copy the `RenderWrap` class. I didn't manage to implement it on top of the current `Wrap` implementation. Therefore  the package is not easy to maintain, since every change to `Wrap` would have to be cherry picked.

Since the feature seems to be a common request when working with a `Wrap`, I think it should be a part of flutter and not a separate package. I just published the package, since I'm using the new `Wrap` in a few projects since October and using a custom flutter version all the time is not pretty and there was no ETA.

If the flutter developers decide that this is not something that should be a part of flutter, then, even though I would not agree, I am fine with closing this PR, but I don't think maintaining the package will be feasible in the long run.

Furthermore, this PR actually simplifies the `RenderWrap` class by avoiding duplicate code. (9b67d01660c3e6fc68dccb9fa0f3d5110488430f)

Therefore this PR
1. adds something the developers want
2. improves the code quality

Maybe I'm biased, but I don't see why this PR shouldn't be merged.

Any updates[^1] on this PR? рҹӨ”
